title: Subsets II
description: Given an integer array nums that may contain duplicates, return all possible subsets (the power set). The solution set must not contain duplicate subsets. Return the solution in any order.
thinking_questions: With duplicates, what makes two subsets the same?
After sorting, duplicate values are adjacent. How does that help you skip duplicates?
When should you skip a duplicate — only at the top level of a recursion depth, not deeper?

hints.L1: Sort to bring duplicates together. Skip duplicate elements at the same recursion level to avoid duplicate subsets.
hints.L2: Same as Subsets I but: sort first, and in the loop skip if i>start and nums[i]==nums[i-1].
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: sort + skip duplicates at same recursion level (i>start and nums[i]==nums[i-1])
nums.sort()
result = []
def backtrack(start, current):
    result.append(current[:])
    for i in range(start, len(nums)):
        if i > start and nums[i] == nums[i-1]: continue  # skip dup
        current.append(nums[i])
        backtrack(i+1, current)
        current.pop()
backtrack(0, [])
return result
# Why i>start: if i==start, this is first use at this level — always try it
# If i>start and same as prev, we'd create a duplicate subset → skip

follow_up_questions: Explain why the condition is `i > start` rather than `i > 0` — give a concrete example showing the difference.
How does this duplicate-skipping pattern apply to Combination Sum II and Permutations II?
Why must the array be sorted for this approach to work?
solution_notes: Sort + skip if i>start and nums[i]==nums[i-1]. Key: i>start (not i>0) — same element can still be first pick at this level.
